Math401Math 401, Fall 2025: Thesis notesMath 401, Fall 2025: Thesis notes, S5, Differential Forms

Math 401, Fall 2025: Thesis notes, S5, Differential Forms

This note aim to investigate What is homology and cohomology?

To answer this question, it’s natural to revisit some concepts we have in Calc III. Particularly, Stoke’s Theorem and De Rham Theorem.

Recall that the Stock’s theorem states that:

∫cdω=∫∂cω\int_c d\omega=\int_{\partial c} \omega∫c​dω=∫∂c​ω

Where ∂c\partial c∂c is a closed curve and ω\omegaω is a 1-form.

What is form means here?

This section is based on extension for conversation with Professor Feres on [11/12/2025].

Differential Forms and applications

Main reference: Differential Forms and its applications 

Differential Forms in our sweet home, Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn

Note

I’m a bit deviated form the notation we used in the book, in the actual text, they use Rpn\mathbb{R}^n_pRpn​ to represent the tangent space of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn at ppp. But to help you link those concepts as we see in smooth manifolds, TpMT_pMTp​M, we will use TpRnT_p\mathbb{R}^nTp​Rn to represent the tangent space of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn at ppp.

Let ppp be a point in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn. The tangent space of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn at ppp is denoted by TpRnT_p\mathbb{R}^nTp​Rn, is the set of all vectors in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn that use ppp as origin.

Definition of a vector field

A vector field is a map that associates to each point ppp in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn a vector v(p)v(p)v(p) in TpRnT_p\mathbb{R}^nTp​Rn.

That is

v(p)=a1(p)e1+...+an(p)env(p)=a_1(p)e_1+...+a_n(p)e_nv(p)=a1​(p)e1​+...+an​(p)en​

where e1,...,ene_1,...,e_ne1​,...,en​ is the standard basis of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, (in fact could be anything you like)

And ai(p)a_i(p)ai​(p) is a function that maps Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn to R\mathbb{R}R.

vvv is differentiable at ppp if the function aia_iai​ is differentiable at ppp.

This gives a vector field vvv on Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.

Definition of dual space of tangent space

To each tangent space TpRnT_p\mathbb{R}^nTp​Rn we can associate the dual space (TpRn)∗(T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*(Tp​Rn)∗, the set of all linear maps from TpRnT_p\mathbb{R}^nTp​Rn to R\mathbb{R}R. (φ:TpRn→R\varphi:T_p\mathbb{R}^n\to \mathbb{R}φ:Tp​Rn→R)

The basis for (TpRn)∗(T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*(Tp​Rn)∗ is obtained by taking (dxi)p(dx_i)_p(dxi​)p​ for i=1,...,ni=1,...,ni=1,...,n.

This is the dual basis for {(ei)p}\{(e_i)_p\}{(ei​)p​} since.

(dxi)p(ej)=∂xi∂xj={0 if i≠j1 if i=j(dx_i)_p(e_j)=\frac{\partial x_i}{\partial x_j}=\begin{cases}0 \text{ if } i\neq j\\ 1 \text{ if } i=j \end{cases}(dxi​)p​(ej​)=∂xj​∂xi​​={0 if i=j1 if i=j​

Definition of a 1-form

A 1-form is a linear map from (TpRn)∗(T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*(Tp​Rn)∗ to R\mathbb{R}R.

ω(p)=a1(p)(dx1)p+...+an(p)(dxn)p\omega(p)=a_1(p)(dx_1)_p+...+a_n(p)(dx_n)_pω(p)=a1​(p)(dx1​)p​+...+an​(p)(dxn​)p​

where ai(p)a_i(p)ai​(p) is a function that maps Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn to R\mathbb{R}R.

Generalization of 1-form is kkk-form defined as follows:

Definition of a kkk-form

We can define the set of linear map Λ2(Rpn)∗\Lambda^2(\mathbb{R}^n_p)^*Λ2(Rpn​)∗ where φ\varphiφ maps from (TpRn)∗×...×(TpRn)∗(T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*\times ... \times (T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*(Tp​Rn)∗×...×(Tp​Rn)∗ to R\mathbb{R}R, that are bilinear and alternate (φ(v1,v2)=−φ(v2,v1\varphi(v_1,v_2)=-\varphi(v_2,v_1φ(v1​,v2​)=−φ(v2​,v1​).

when φ1\varphi_1φ1​ and φ2\varphi_2φ2​ are linear maps from (TpRn)∗(T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*(Tp​Rn)∗ to R\mathbb{R}R, then φ1∧φ2\varphi_1\wedge \varphi_2φ1​∧φ2​ is a bilinear map from (TpRn)∗×(TpRn)∗(T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*\times (T_p\mathbb{R}^n)^*(Tp​Rn)∗×(Tp​Rn)∗ to R\mathbb{R}R by setting

(φ1∧φ2)(v1,v2)=φ1(v1)φ2(v2)−φ1(v2)φ2(v1)=det⁡(φi(vj))(\varphi_1\wedge \varphi_2)(v_1,v_2)=\varphi_1(v_1)\varphi_2(v_2)-\varphi_1(v_2)\varphi_2(v_1)=\det(\varphi_i(v_j))(φ1​∧φ2​)(v1​,v2​)=φ1​(v1​)φ2​(v2​)−φ1​(v2​)φ2​(v1​)=det(φi​(vj​))

where i,j=1,…,ki,j=1,\ldots,ki,j=1,…,k, kkk is the degree of the exterior form

More generally, (φ1∧φ2∧⋯∧φk)(v1,v2,…,vk)=det⁡(φi(vj))(\varphi_1\wedge \varphi_2\wedge\dots \wedge \varphi_k)(v_1,v_2,\dots,v_k)=\det(\varphi_i(v_j))(φ1​∧φ2​∧⋯∧φk​)(v1​,v2​,…,vk​)=det(φi​(vj​)).

And {(dxi∧dxj)p,i<j}\{(dx_i\wedge dx_j)_p,i<j\}{(dxi​∧dxj​)p​,i<j} forms a basis for Λ2(Rpn)∗\Lambda^2(\mathbb{R}^n_p)^*Λ2(Rpn​)∗.

  • (dxi∧dxj)p=−(dxj∧dxi)p(dx_i\wedge dx_j)_p=-(dx_j\wedge dx_i)_p(dxi​∧dxj​)p​=−(dxj​∧dxi​)p​
  • (dxi∧dxi)p=0(dx_i\wedge dx_i)_p=0(dxi​∧dxi​)p​=0

An exterior fom of degree 2 in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn is a correspondence ω\omegaω that associates to each point ppp in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn an element ω(p)∈Λ2(Rpn)∗\omega(p)\in \Lambda^2(\mathbb{R}^n_p)^*ω(p)∈Λ2(Rpn​)∗.

That is

ω(p)=a12(p)(dx1∧dx2)p+a13(p)(dx1∧dx3)p+a23(p)(dx2∧dx3)p\omega(p)=a_{12}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_2)_p+a_{13}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_3)_p+a_{23}(p)(dx_2\wedge dx_3)_pω(p)=a12​(p)(dx1​∧dx2​)p​+a13​(p)(dx1​∧dx3​)p​+a23​(p)(dx2​∧dx3​)p​

In the case of R3\mathbb{R}^3R3.

Example for real space 4 product

0-forms: functino in R4\mathbb{R}^4R4

1-forms: a1(p)(dx1)p+a2(p)(dx2)p+a3(p)(dx3)p+a4(p)(dx4)pa_1(p)(dx_1)_p+a_2(p)(dx_2)_p+a_3(p)(dx_3)_p+a_4(p)(dx_4)_pa1​(p)(dx1​)p​+a2​(p)(dx2​)p​+a3​(p)(dx3​)p​+a4​(p)(dx4​)p​

2-forms: a12(p)(dx1∧dx2)p+a13(p)(dx1∧dx3)p+a14(p)(dx1∧dx4)p+a23(p)(dx2∧dx3)p+a24(p)(dx2∧dx4)p+a34(p)(dx3∧dx4)pa_{12}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_2)_p+a_{13}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_3)_p+a_{14}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_4)_p+a_{23}(p)(dx_2\wedge dx_3)_p+a_{24}(p)(dx_2\wedge dx_4)_p+a_{34}(p)(dx_3\wedge dx_4)_pa12​(p)(dx1​∧dx2​)p​+a13​(p)(dx1​∧dx3​)p​+a14​(p)(dx1​∧dx4​)p​+a23​(p)(dx2​∧dx3​)p​+a24​(p)(dx2​∧dx4​)p​+a34​(p)(dx3​∧dx4​)p​

3-forms: a123(p)(dx1∧dx2∧dx3)p+a124(p)(dx1∧dx2∧dx4)p+a134(p)(dx1∧dx3∧dx4)p+a234(p)(dx2∧dx3∧dx4)pa_{123}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_2\wedge dx_3)_p+a_{124}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_2\wedge dx_4)_p+a_{134}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_3\wedge dx_4)_p+a_{234}(p)(dx_2\wedge dx_3\wedge dx_4)_pa123​(p)(dx1​∧dx2​∧dx3​)p​+a124​(p)(dx1​∧dx2​∧dx4​)p​+a134​(p)(dx1​∧dx3​∧dx4​)p​+a234​(p)(dx2​∧dx3​∧dx4​)p​

4-forms: a1234(p)(dx1∧dx2∧dx3∧dx4)pa_{1234}(p)(dx_1\wedge dx_2\wedge dx_3\wedge dx_4)_pa1234​(p)(dx1​∧dx2​∧dx3​∧dx4​)p​

Exterior product of forms

Let ω=∑aIdxI\omega=\sum a_{I}dx_Iω=∑aI​dxI​ be a k form where I=(i1,i2,…,ik)I=(i_1,i_2,\ldots,i_k)I=(i1​,i2​,…,ik​) and i1<i2<⋯<iki_1<i_2<\cdots<i_ki1​<i2​<⋯<ik​.

φ∧ω=∑bjdxj\varphi\wedge \omega=\sum b_jdx_jφ∧ω=∑bj​dxj​ be a s form where j=(j1,j2,…,js)j=(j_1,j_2,\ldots,j_s)j=(j1​,j2​,…,js​) and j1<j2<⋯<jsj_1<j_2<\cdots<j_sj1​<j2​<⋯<js​.

The exterior product is defined as

(φ∧ω)(v1,…,vk)=∑IJaIbJdxI∧dxJ(\varphi\wedge \omega)(v_1,\ldots,v_k)=\sum_{IJ}a_I b_J dx_I\wedge dx_J(φ∧ω)(v1​,…,vk​)=IJ∑​aI​bJ​dxI​∧dxJ​

Example for exterior product of forms

Let ω=x1dx1+x2dx2+x3dx3\omega=x_1dx_1+x_2dx_2+x_3dx_3ω=x1​dx1​+x2​dx2​+x3​dx3​ be a 1-form in R3\mathbb{R}^3R3 and φ=x1dx1∧dx1∧dx3\varphi=x_1dx_1\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_3φ=x1​dx1​∧dx1​∧dx3​ be a 2-form in R3\mathbb{R}^3R3.

Then

ω∧φ=x2dx2∧dx1∧dx3+x3x1dx3∧dx1∧dx2=(x1x3−x2)dx1∧dx2∧dx3\begin{aligned} \omega\wedge \varphi&=x_2 dx_2\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_3+x_3x_1 dx_3\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_2\\ &=(x_1x_3-x_2)dx_1\wedge dx_2\wedge dx_3 \end{aligned}ω∧φ​=x2​dx2​∧dx1​∧dx3​+x3​x1​dx3​∧dx1​∧dx2​=(x1​x3​−x2​)dx1​∧dx2​∧dx3​​

Note dx1∧dx1=0dx_1\wedge dx_1=0dx1​∧dx1​=0 therefore dx1∧dx1∧dx3=0dx_1\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_3=0dx1​∧dx1​∧dx3​=0

Additional properties of exterior product

Let ω\omegaω be a kkk form, φ\varphiφ be a sss form, and θ\thetaθ be an rrr form.

  • (ω∧φ)∧θ=ω∧(φ∧θ)(\omega\wedge\varphi)\wedge\theta=\omega\wedge(\varphi\wedge\theta)(ω∧φ)∧θ=ω∧(φ∧θ)
  • (ω∧φ)=(−1)k+s(φ∧ω)(\omega\wedge\varphi)=(-1)^{k+s}(\varphi\wedge\omega)(ω∧φ)=(−1)k+s(φ∧ω)
  • ω∧(φ+θ)=ω∧φ+ω∧θ\omega\wedge(\varphi+\theta)=\omega\wedge\varphi+\omega\wedge\thetaω∧(φ+θ)=ω∧φ+ω∧θ

Important implications with differential maps

Let f:Rn→Rmf:\mathbb{R}^n\to \mathbb{R}^mf:Rn→Rm be a differentiable map. Then fff induces a map f∗f^*f∗ from k-forms in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn to k-forms in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm.

That is

(f∗ω)(p)(v1,…,vk)=ω(f(p))(df(p)1v1,…,df(p)kvk)(f^*\omega)(p)(v_1,\ldots,v_k)=\omega(f(p))(df(p)_1v_1,\ldots,df(p)_kv_k)(f∗ω)(p)(v1​,…,vk​)=ω(f(p))(df(p)1​v1​,…,df(p)k​vk​)

Here p∈Rnp\in \mathbb{R}^np∈Rn, v1,…,vk∈TpRnv_1,\ldots,v_k\in T_p\mathbb{R}^nv1​,…,vk​∈Tp​Rn, and df(p):TpRn→Tf(p)Rmdf(p):T_p\mathbb{R}^n\to T_{f(p)}\mathbb{R}^mdf(p):Tp​Rn→Tf(p)​Rm.

If ggg is a 0-form, we have

f∗(g)=g∘ff^*(g)=g\circ ff∗(g)=g∘f

Additional properties for differential maps

Let f:Rn→Rmf:\mathbb{R}^n\to \mathbb{R}^mf:Rn→Rm be a differentiable map, ω,φ\omega,\varphiω,φ be k-forms on Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm and g:Rm→Rg:\mathbb{R}^m\to \mathbb{R}g:Rm→R be a 0-form on Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm. Then:

  • f∗(ω+φ)=f∗ω+f∗φf^*(\omega+\varphi)=f^*\omega+f^*\varphif∗(ω+φ)=f∗ω+f∗φ
  • f∗(gω)=f∗(g)f∗ωf^*(g\omega)=f^*(g)f^*\omegaf∗(gω)=f∗(g)f∗ω
  • If φ1,…,φk\varphi_1,\dots,\varphi_kφ1​,…,φk​ are 1-forms in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm, f∗(φ1∧⋯∧φk)=f∗φ1∧⋯∧f∗φkf^*(\varphi_1\wedge\cdots\wedge\varphi_k)=f^*\varphi_1\wedge\cdots\wedge f^*\varphi_kf∗(φ1​∧⋯∧φk​)=f∗φ1​∧⋯∧f∗φk​

If g:Rp→Rng:\mathbb{R}^p\to \mathbb{R}^ng:Rp→Rn is a differential map and φ,ω\varphi,\omegaφ,ω are any two-forms in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm.

  • f∗(ω∧φ)=f∗ω∧f∗φf^*(\omega\wedge\varphi)=f^*\omega\wedge f^*\varphif∗(ω∧φ)=f∗ω∧f∗φ
  • (f∘g)∗omega=g∗(f∗ω)(f\circ g)^*omega=g^*(f^*\omega)(f∘g)∗omega=g∗(f∗ω)

Exterior Differential

Let ω=∑aIdxI\omega=\sum a_{I}dx_Iω=∑aI​dxI​ be a k form in mathbbRnmathbb{R}^nmathbbRn. The exterior differential dωd\omegadω of ω\omegaω is defined by

dω=∑daI∧dxId\omega=\sum da_{I}\wedge dx_Idω=∑daI​∧dxI​

Additional properties of exterior differential

  • d(ω1+ω2)=dω1+dω2d(\omega_1+\omega_2)=d\omega_1+d\omega_2d(ω1​+ω2​)=dω1​+dω2​ where ω1,ω2\omega_1,\omega_2ω1​,ω2​ are k-forms
  • d(ω∧φ)=dω∧φ+(−1)kw∧dφd(\omega\wedge\varphi)=d\omega\wedge\varphi+(-1)^kw\wedge d\varphid(ω∧φ)=dω∧φ+(−1)kw∧dφ where ω\omegaω is a k-form and φ\varphiφ is a s-form
  • d(dω)=d2ω=0d(d\omega)=d^2\omega=0d(dω)=d2ω=0
  • d(f∗ω)=f∗dωd(f^*\omega)=f^*d\omegad(f∗ω)=f∗dω where fff is a differentiable map and ω\omegaω is a k-form

Differentiable manifolds

A different flavor of differential manifolds

Definition of differentiable manifold

An nnn-dimensional differentiable manifold is a set MMM together with a family of of injective maps fα:Uα⊆Rn→Mf_\alpha:U_\alpha\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\to Mfα​:Uα​⊆Rn→M of open sets UαU_\alphaUα​ in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn in to MMM such that:

  • ⋃αfα(Uα)=M\bigcup_\alpha f_\alpha(U_\alpha)=M⋃α​fα​(Uα​)=M
  • For each pair α,β\alpha,\betaα,β, with fα(Uα)∩fβ(Uβ)=W≠∅f_\alpha(U_\alpha)\cap f_\beta(U_\beta)=W\neq \emptysetfα​(Uα​)∩fβ​(Uβ​)=W=∅, the sets fα−1(W)f_\alpha^{-1}(W)fα−1​(W) and fβ−1(W)f_\beta^{-1}(W)fβ−1​(W) are open sets in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn and the maps fβ−1∘fαf_\beta^{-1}\circ f_\alphafβ−1​∘fα​ and fα−1∘fβf_\alpha^{-1}\circ f_\betafα−1​∘fβ​ are differentiable.
  • The family {(Uα,fα)}\{(U_\alpha,f_\alpha)\}{(Uα​,fα​)} is the maximal relative to the two properties above.

This condition is weaker than smooth manifold, in smooth manifold, we require the function to be class of C∞C^\inftyC∞ (continuous differentiable of all order), now we only needs it to be differentiable.

Definition of differentiable map between differentiable manifolds

Let M1nM_1^nM1n​ and M2MM_2^MM2M​ be differentiable manifolds. A map φ:M1→M2\varphi:M_1\to M_2φ:M1​→M2​ is a differentiable at a point p∈M1p\in M_1p∈M1​ if given a parameterization g:V⊂Rm→M2g:V\subset \mathbb{R}^m\to M_2g:V⊂Rm→M2​ around φ(p)\varphi(p)φ(p), there exists a parameterization f:U⊆Rn→M1f:U\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n\to M_1f:U⊆Rn→M1​ around ppp such that:

φ(f(U))⊂g(V)\varphi(f(U))\subset g(V)φ(f(U))⊂g(V) and the map

g−1∘φ∘f:U⊂Rn→Rmg^{-1}\circ \varphi\circ f: U\subset \mathbb{R}^n\to \mathbb{R}^mg−1∘φ∘f:U⊂Rn→Rm

is differentiable at f−1(p)f^{-1}(p)f−1(p).

It is differentiable in an open set of M1M_1M1​ if it is differentiable at all points in such set.

The map g−1∘φ∘fg^{-1}\circ \varphi\circ fg−1∘φ∘f is the expression of parameterization of fff and ggg. (Since the change of parameterization is differentiable, the property that fff is differentiable does not depends on the choice of parameterization.)

Tangent vector over differentiable curve

Let α:I→M\alpha: I\to Mα:I→M be a differentiable curve on a differentiable manifold MMM, with α(0)=p∈M\alpha(0)=p\in Mα(0)=p∈M, and let DDD be the set of functions of MMM which are differentiable at ppp. then tangent vector to the curve α\alphaα at ppp is the map α′(0):D→R\alpha'(0):D\to \mathbb{R}α′(0):D→R given by

α′(0)φ=ddt(φ⋅α(t))∣t=0\alpha'(0)\varphi=\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi\cdot \alpha(t))|_{t=0}α′(0)φ=dtd​(φ⋅α(t))∣t=0​

A tangent vector at p∈Mp\in Mp∈M is the
